threats.title: Delegation Checkpoint Wipe-Out via Single Token Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: In VotingDelegationLib.moveTokenDelegates, the source delegate filtering uses `ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep` to decide which tokens to keep. When a user who has delegated to address C transfers a single token, ALL tokens delegated to C by ANY user whose tokens are not owned by C are wiped from C's checkpoint. An attacker can wipe a major delegate's governance voting power by creating a minimal lock, delegating to the target, then transferring that token away.
threats.investigation_rationale: The filtering condition is fundamentally wrong: it retains tokens where ownerOf(tId) == delegate_address, but delegates rarely OWN the tokens delegated to them. This means on any transfer by a delegator, ALL other delegators' tokens are also removed from the delegate's checkpoint. This is a direct governance manipulation vector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep used to filter tokens to keep; should be tId != _tokenId. When srcRep is a delegate (not the owner), no owned tokens match, wiping all entries.

preconditions: Target delegate C has voting power delegated from at least one other user
Attacker can create a minimal veNFT lock
Attacker calls delegate(C) then transferFrom to a second address

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: ownerOfFn(tId) == srcRep — filters by ownership not token identity
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Create lock → delegate to target → transfer token. All other delegators' tokens removed from target's checkpoint
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker wipes governance voting power of a major delegate, enabling hostile governance proposals to pass or preventing quorum

economic_questions: How much delegated voting power can be wiped in a single transaction?
Can the attacker profit from governance manipulation (e.g., pass proposal to extract funds)?
Does this affect gauge voting weights through getPastVotes?
validation_requirements: Verify that _isCheckpointInNewBlock path correctly reproduces the bug when srcRep != owner
Test with multiple delegators to same delegate to confirm all are wiped
Confirm getVotes returns near-zero after attack for the target delegate

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal: 1 token worth of BLACK for a tiny lock
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.01 AVAX gas for create_lock + delegate + transferFrom
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: governance control worth potentially entire protocol TVL
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near-infinite ROI if governance proposal extraction is possible

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None — only needs minimal token amount
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; pure on-chain governance manipulation

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users who delegated to the targeted delegate
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of delegated voting power lost from checkpoint
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All delegated governance power in the system

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check _isCheckpointInNewBlock logic and same-block vs new-block paths
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace full checkpoint state before/after transfer with delegated tokens

threats.title: AVM-Managed Token Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked in AutoVotingEscrow Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: Bribe.getReward sends rewards to ownerOf(tokenId). For AVM-managed tokens, ownerOf returns the AutoVotingEscrow instance (not the manager). The check `_owner == avm` compares against the AVM Manager address, which is always false. Rewards are sent to the AutoVotingEscrow contract which has no token recovery function, permanently locking them. Additionally, setOriginalOwner is a no-op (empty function body), so even the fallback path is broken.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a compound failure: (1) address comparison mismatch between AVE instance and AVM manager, (2) setOriginalOwner never writes to storage, (3) VE._transferFrom checks `_to == avm` but actual recipient is AVE not AVM. All AVM users' bribe rewards are permanently lost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setOriginalOwner has empty function body — originalOwner mapping never written. In Bribes.sol getReward, _owner == avm compares AVE address against manager address (always false).

preconditions: User has enabled auto-voting via enableAutoVoting
Bribe rewards exist for the user's voted pools
gaugeManager calls getReward for AVM-managed tokenIds

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: ownerOf(tokenId) returns AutoVotingEscrow address; `if(_owner == avm)` where avm = AutoVotingEscrowManager address → false
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: No manipulation needed; all AVM bribe rewards are permanently locked by code defect
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Total bribe rewards for all AVM-managed tokens are permanently lost

economic_questions: What percentage of total voting power is managed by AVM?
What is the total bribe reward volume at risk per epoch?
Are there any emergency recovery paths for locked rewards?
validation_requirements: Verify IERC20.safeTransfer to AVE contract address succeeds (tokens are locked there)
Confirm AutoVotingEscrow has no withdrawERC20 or recovery function
Check if bribeFactory owner can use emergencyRecoverERC20 as mitigation

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 — happens automatically to all AVM users
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All AVM bribe rewards per epoch permanently lost
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Ongoing for every epoch with AVM-managed tokens

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Users lose trust in AVM system; potential protocol reputation damage

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All bribe rewards proportional to AVM voting power

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Trace ownerOf path for AVM-managed tokenIds through Bribe.getReward
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify safeTransfer destination and AutoVotingEscrow lacks recovery

threats.title: Bribe Reward Gas DoS Due to Unbounded Epoch Loop with WEEK=1800
threats.hypothesis_description: Bribe.earned() iterates through every epoch since lastEarn with no iteration cap. With WEEK=1800 seconds (30 minutes), after just 7 real-world days there are 336 epochs to iterate. Each iteration reads multiple storage slots from checkpoints. This easily exceeds block gas limits, making getReward() permanently revert for users who haven't claimed recently.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike standard ve(3,3) implementations using WEEK=7 days, this protocol uses WEEK=1800 seconds. The earned() loop was designed for weekly epochs and becomes DoS-prone with 30-minute epochs. Even moderate claim delays cause irrecoverable reward lockup.

proof_of_signal.pattern: numEpochs = (epochStart(block.timestamp) - _currTs) / WEEK with no upper bound on loop iterations; WEEK=1800 means 336 epochs per 7 days

preconditions: User has voted and earned bribe rewards
User has not claimed for several days
WEEK = 1800 (confirmed in BlackTimeLibrary)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: numEpochs = (epochStart(now) - lastEarn) / 1800. After 7 days: 336 iterations, each reading 2+ storage slots
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural passage of time without claims causes permanent reward lockup; attacker can also grief by depositing tiny bribes to force reward accumulation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Victims' locked rewards become protocol surplus; if emergencyRecoverERC20 is used, owner extracts them

economic_questions: At how many epochs does earned() exceed the block gas limit on Avalanche?
What percentage of bribe depositors are likely to experience this?
Can partial claims be implemented as mitigation?
validation_requirements: Gas-profile the earned() function with 100, 200, 500 epoch iterations
Determine Avalanche C-Chain block gas limit (currently 15M)
Test getReward reverting with realistic claim gaps

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 for natural DoS; minimal for griefing
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All unclaimed bribe rewards for affected users
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent after threshold epoch count reached

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after sufficient epochs pass without claims

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any bribe earner who delays claiming by several days
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of unclaimed bribe rewards permanently locked
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All bribe rewards for inactive claimers

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Gas per iteration in earned() on Avalanche
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Profile exact gas costs with varying epoch counts

threats.title: RewardsDistributor claim_many Sends Rewards to AutoVotingEscrow Instead of Original Owner
threats.hypothesis_description: RewardsDistributor.claim_many does not check AVM ownership for expired locks, sending rebase rewards to ownerOf(tokenId) which returns the AutoVotingEscrow contract address. The single claim() function correctly resolves the original owner via avm.getOriginalOwner(), but claim_many bypasses this check entirely, permanently locking rewards in the AVE contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code-level inconsistency between claim() and claim_many(). Anyone can call claim_many for any tokenId, directing rewards to the wrong address. Combined with the setOriginalOwner no-op, even claim() has issues, but claim_many's omission is more severe since it doesn't even attempt AVM resolution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claim_many uses ownerOf(_tokenId) directly without AVM check; claim() at line 108-125 has avm.getOriginalOwner check for expired locks

preconditions: Token is managed by AVM (in AutoVotingEscrow)
Lock is expired (non-permanent, end < block.timestamp)
Anyone calls claim_many with the affected tokenIds

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: claim_many: IERC20(token).transfer(IVotingEscrow(_voting_escrow).ownerOf(_tokenId), amount) — ownerOf returns AVE address
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Anyone can call claim_many for any tokenId; rewards for AVM-managed expired locks permanently locked
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All rebase rewards for AVM expired locks are lost via claim_many

economic_questions: How many AVM-managed tokens have expired or will expire?
What is the typical rebase distribution per epoch?
Can an attacker call claim_many before the legitimate user calls claim?
validation_requirements: Verify claim_many routes to AVE address for AVM tokens
Confirm AutoVotingEscrow lacks token withdrawal function
Test race between claim and claim_many for same tokenId

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Griefing: all rebase rewards for AVM expired tokens locked
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A — griefing attack, not direct extraction

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After epoch with significant rebase distribution
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: AVM users lose rebase rewards on expired locks

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Rebase rewards proportional to AVM expired lock voting power

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace claim_many code path for AVM tokenIds

threats.title: delegateBySig Replay Attack Due to Nonce Not Incremented by Direct delegate()
threats.hypothesis_description: The delegate() function does not increment the nonces mapping, so previously signed delegateBySig messages remain valid after a user calls delegate() directly. An attacker who received a valid delegation signature can submit it at any time to override the signatory's subsequent direct delegate() calls, redirecting all of the victim's veNFT governance voting power.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a standard EIP-712 nonce management bug. The nonce is only consumed by delegateBySig but not invalidated by delegate(). Since delegation controls governance voting power, this allows stale signatures to override current delegation preferences.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delegate() calls _delegate() but never increments nonces[msg.sender]. delegateBySig at line 627-657 checks nonce == nonces[signatory]++. Old signatures remain valid after direct delegate() calls.

preconditions: Victim has previously signed a delegateBySig message (e.g., for gasless delegation)
Attacker (or intended delegatee) possesses the signed message
Victim subsequently changed delegation via direct delegate() call

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: delegate() does not increment nonces[]; nonces[signatory]++ only in delegateBySig
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit old delegation signature to override victim's current delegation, redirecting governance voting power
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Control victim's governance votes to pass/block proposals, potentially affecting treasury management

economic_questions: Is delegateBySig commonly used in this protocol?
What governance decisions are controlled by delegated votes?
Can accumulated voting power from hijacked delegations pass critical proposals?
validation_requirements: Confirm delegate() does not modify nonces
Test replay of old signature after direct delegate() call
Verify delegated votes affect governance outcomes

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 — only need a previously signed message
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: governance power over protocol treasury

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who signed delegateBySig and later changed delegation

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if protocol uses delegateBySig in production
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify nonce state across delegate and delegateBySig calls

threats.title: Expired Lock Merge into SMNFT Grants Free Bonus Voting Power
threats.hypothesis_description: The merge function does not check that the source token has an active lock for non-permanent sources. An expired non-permanent lock can be merged into an SMNFT target, burning the expired tokens and granting SMNFT bonus voting power (10% by default). The user effectively converts withdrawable tokens into permanent voting power with a free bonus, when the intent is that SMNFT conversions require commitment (non-expired tokens).
threats.investigation_rationale: The merge function only checks `_locked1.end > block.timestamp || _locked1.isPermanent` for the destination, not the source. Users can merge expired locks to gain bonus voting power without the intended time-lock commitment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only _locked1 (destination) checked for expiration: require(_locked1.end > block.timestamp || _locked1.isPermanent). No corresponding check for _locked0 (source) when !_locked0.isPermanent.

preconditions: User owns an expired normal veNFT (source) and an SMNFT (destination)
Source lock not voted or attached
SMNFT_BONUS > 0 (default 1000/10000 = 10%)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: User can withdraw expired 1000 tokens OR merge into SMNFT for 1000 + 100 bonus = 1100 voting power permanently
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Let locks expire intentionally, then merge into SMNFT for 10% free bonus
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Free 10% voting power bonus on all expired tokens merged

economic_questions: What is the economic value of 10% permanent voting power bonus vs token value?
How many users could exploit this to accumulate disproportionate voting power?
Does this distort gauge voting and bribe incentives?
validation_requirements: Confirm merge succeeds with expired source lock
Verify smNFTBalance and bonus calculations
Check if the burned tokens are properly accounted

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 10% bonus voting power on all expired tokens
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 10% permanent voting power gain per conversion

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Gradual inflation of SMNFT voting power

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other veNFT holders via diluted voting power
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional dilution based on attacker's merged amount

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test merge with expired source into SMNFT destination

threats.title: SMNFT_BONUS Change Breaks TotalSupply-Individual Balance Consistency
threats.hypothesis_description: When team calls setSmNFTBonus to change the bonus rate, the global point_history immediately uses calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) with the NEW rate, while individual SMNFT checkpoints retain OLD stored smNFTBonus values. This creates a divergence where totalSupply() != sum(balanceOfNFT(i)), affecting RewardsDistributor share calculations and governance quorum determination.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _checkpoint function sets last_point.smNFTBonus = calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) using the current SMNFT_BONUS rate. Individual user points store their smNFTBonus at creation time. After a bonus change, global and individual accounting diverge, distorting all proportional calculations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: last_point.smNFTBonus = calculate_sm_nft_bonus(smNFTBalance) uses current SMNFT_BONUS rate, but individual points store old smNFTBonus values

preconditions: Team changes SMNFT_BONUS via setSmNFTBonus
Existing SMNFT tokens have old bonus values stored in checkpoints
Any operation triggers a global checkpoint

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: If bonus increases: totalSupply inflates (higher smNFTBonus globally) while individual balanceOfNFT stays at old rate → sum(individual) < totalSupply
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After bonus increase, RewardsDistributor: each user's share = balanceOfNFT / totalSupply is reduced because totalSupply is inflated
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users who checkpoint their tokens after bonus change get higher individual balance, capturing disproportionate rebase rewards

economic_questions: What is the magnitude of divergence for realistic bonus changes?
How does this affect RewardsDistributor claim calculations?
Can a user strategically checkpoint after bonus change to capture extra rewards?
validation_requirements: Compare totalSupply vs sum of individual balances after bonus change
Calculate reward distribution skew from the divergence
Test whether early checkpointers gain advantage over late ones

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for deposit_for or poke to trigger checkpoint
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to bonus change magnitude × user's SMNFT balance
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: If bonus doubles, up to 2x reward share advantage

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All SMNFT holders and non-SMNFT holders competing for rebase
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced rebase rewards proportional to divergence

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model divergence for various bonus changes

threats.title: Checkpoint Loop Insufficient for 1800s Epoch Duration Causing Stale Global Voting Power
threats.hypothesis_description: The _checkpoint function iterates up to 255 weeks (255 * WEEK). With WEEK=1800 seconds, this covers only 255*1800 = 459,000 seconds (~5.3 days). If no user interaction triggers a checkpoint for more than 5.3 days, the global point_history becomes permanently stale, causing incorrect totalSupply calculations. Similarly, _checkpoint_token in RewardsDistributor iterates max 20 times covering only 10 hours.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checkpoint iteration limits were designed for WEEK=604800 (7 days), not WEEK=1800. Protocol inactivity periods exceeding 5.3 days break global accounting. RewardsDistributor's 20-iteration limit breaks after just 10 hours of inactivity.
threats.config_evidence: BlackTimeLibrary.sol: WEEK = 1800; VotingEscrow._checkpoint loop: 255 iterations; RewardsDistributor._checkpoint_token: 20 iterations

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = 0; i < 255; ++i) with t_i += WEEK where WEEK=1800, covers 255*1800=459000 seconds max

preconditions: No VotingEscrow interaction for >5.3 days (checkpoint gap)
Or no RewardsDistributor checkpoint_token for >10 hours
Protocol exists on Avalanche where activity may be intermittent

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: 255 * 1800 = 459000 seconds ≈ 5.3 days max catch-up; 20 * 1800 = 36000 seconds = 10 hours for RewardsDistributor
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Wait for inactivity gap, then exploit stale totalSupply to claim disproportionate rebase rewards
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Stale totalSupply inflated → individual shares of rebase pool are incorrect

economic_questions: How likely is a 5.3-day inactivity gap on Avalanche?
How much does stale totalSupply diverge from reality?
Can an attacker force-create an inactivity gap?
validation_requirements: Simulate checkpoint after 6-day gap to verify incomplete catch-up
Measure totalSupply error magnitude after stale period
Test RewardsDistributor distribution accuracy after 12-hour checkpoint gap

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for claim after stale period
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Excess rebase proportional to totalSupply error
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable — depends on duration of staleness

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All rebase recipients during stale period

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model totalSupply divergence over various gap durations

threats.title: Bribe Withdraw Silent Skip Creates Phantom Voting Weight for Reward Extraction
threats.hypothesis_description: Bribe.withdraw silently skips withdrawal when amount > balanceOf[tokenId] instead of reverting. During VoterV3._reset, if the vote weight for a pool exceeds the bribe's recorded balance for that tokenId (due to any accounting edge case), the withdrawal is silently skipped, leaving stale checkpoint data. The user retains phantom voting weight in the bribe contract, earning rewards they shouldn't receive.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId])` guard silently succeeds without modifying state when the condition is false. This is a defensive code pattern that masks accounting inconsistencies rather than catching them. Any path that creates a mismatch between VoterV3.votes and Bribe.balanceOf enables reward theft.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId]) { ... } — silently does nothing when amount > balance, leaving stale checkpoint and totalSupply intact

preconditions: Any code path creates mismatch between VoterV3 votes and Bribe balanceOf
VoterV3._reset attempts to withdraw more than Bribe records
User retains phantom balance in bribe earning pro-rata rewards

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: withdraw(amount, tokenId) where amount > balanceOf[tokenId] → no state change, totalSupply unchanged
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Phantom balanceOf in bribe earns rewards proportionally
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User claims rewards based on retained phantom balance after vote reset

economic_questions: Can a concrete path create amount > balanceOf mismatch?
How much extra reward can be extracted per epoch?
Does the totalSupply inflation dilute legitimate earners?
validation_requirements: Enumerate all code paths that could create vote/bribe balance mismatch
Test poke after gauge is killed and revived to check for mismatch
Verify totalSupply tracks correctly through all edge cases

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Pro-rata share of bribe rewards based on phantom balance
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persistent across epochs until bribe accounting is corrected

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After gauge kill/revive or other state change

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Legitimate bribe earners in the same pool
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced reward share proportional to phantom balance

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace balanceOf through all vote/reset/poke sequences

threats.title: PermissionsRegistry.removeRole Corrupts Role Tracking Data Enabling Permission Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: PermissionsRegistry.removeRole uses `_roles[_roles.length - 1]` (global roles array) instead of `_addressToRoles[rta[i]][_addressToRoles[rta[i]].length - 1]` for the swap-and-pop operation on per-address role tracking. This writes unrelated role strings into users' _addressToRoles arrays. While hasRole mapping is correctly cleared, the corrupted _addressToRoles can cause subsequent removeRoleFrom to malfunction, potentially leaving residual role assignments.
threats.investigation_rationale: The swap-and-pop uses the wrong source array. After removeRole, users' role arrays contain stale entries from the global _roles array. If removeRoleFrom is later called on these corrupted entries, the keccak256 comparison may match incorrectly, causing wrong roles to be removed or double-pops.
preconditions: blackMultisig calls removeRole for any role
At least one address has the role being removed
Subsequent removeRoleFrom operations on affected addresses

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Corrupted role arrays cause removeRoleFrom to remove wrong roles, potentially keeping unauthorized roles active
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Addresses retain roles they should have lost, maintaining unauthorized protocol access

economic_questions: Can corrupted role data lead to unauthorized gauge creation or fee changes?
What is the financial impact of stale VOTER_ADMIN or GOVERNANCE roles?
Is the loop's lack of break after pop exploitable for additional corruption?
validation_requirements: Simulate removeRole with multiple role holders
Verify _addressToRoles state after removal
Test subsequent removeRoleFrom on corrupted entries

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on retained unauthorized role capabilities
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially high if GOVERNANCE role is retained

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Entire protocol if unauthorized roles persist
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Depends on actions taken with retained roles
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Full protocol TVL if GOVERNANCE access is retained

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current role assignments and _roles array state
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Step through removeRole with concrete data

threats.title: SMNFT Split Does Not Update smNFTBalance Causing Global Accounting Divergence
threats.hypothesis_description: When splitting an SMNFT, the split function does not adjust smNFTBalance. The original SMNFT is burned and two new ones are created, but the global smNFTBalance tracker remains unchanged. While this is correct if no bonus rate changes occur, if SMNFT_BONUS changes between creation and split, the two new tokens' calculate_original_sm_nft_amount results using the current rate will sum to a different value than the original's real amount, breaking INV-STATE-006.
threats.investigation_rationale: The split function uses current SMNFT_BONUS to calculate _splitAmount for SMNFTs. If bonus changed since creation, the split amounts are calculated with the new rate, creating tokens whose 'original amounts' (via calculate_original_sm_nft_amount) don't sum to the original's original amount. smNFTBalance becomes inconsistent with actual holdings.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _splitAmount = int128(int256(_amount + calculate_sm_nft_bonus(_amount))) uses current SMNFT_BONUS; smNFTBalance is never adjusted in split

preconditions: SMNFT_BONUS has been changed via setSmNFTBonus since the SMNFT was created
canSplit[msg.sender] or canSplit[address(0)] is true
User splits an SMNFT

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: SMNFT created with bonus=10%: real=100, locked=110. Bonus changed to 20%. Split with _amount=50: _splitAmount=50+10=60. Token1=50, Token2=60. original_amounts: 50*10000/12000 + 60*10000/12000 ≈ 91.67 ≠ 100
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Repeated split-merge cycles after bonus change can create or destroy effective smNFTBalance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Divergence between smNFTBalance and actual SMNFT holdings affects global totalSupply and reward distribution

economic_questions: How large can the smNFTBalance divergence grow?
Does this affect totalSupply calculation and rebase distribution?
Can repeated split-merge amplify the divergence?
validation_requirements: Calculate smNFTBalance divergence for various bonus changes and split scenarios
Test split-merge cycles to check for amplification
Verify impact on _supply_at calculations

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Excess rebase rewards from inflated totalSupply

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: SMNFT_BONUS=1000, PRECISION=10000; model various change scenarios
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace smNFTBalance through split with changed bonus

threats.title: BlackClaims recoverERC20 Can Drain Active Season Reward Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: BlackClaims.recoverERC20 allows the owner to withdraw the FULL balance of ANY token including the reward token (BLACK) during an active claiming season. There is no restriction preventing withdrawal of the reward token. This directly violates the solvency invariant INV-ECON-006 since remaining_reward_amount could exceed the actual contract balance after recovery, making legitimate claims revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function `IERC20(tokenAddress_).transfer(msg.sender, _balance)` sends the entire balance to the owner. If tokenAddress_ is the reward token (BLACK) and a season is active with unclaimed rewards, all subsequent claimAndStakeReward calls will fail due to insufficient balance. Unlike emergencyRecoverERC20 in Bribes which has an explicit warning comment, BlackClaims has no safeguard.

proof_of_signal.pattern: recoverERC20 transfers full balance with no check against remaining_reward_amount; token address not restricted from reward token

preconditions: Owner (or secondOwner) calls recoverERC20 with reward token address
Active season with remaining unclaimed rewards
Owner acts maliciously or makes an error

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner drains reward token balance; _preClaim subtraction _season.remaining_reward_amount -= _reward underflows or transfer fails
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Owner extracts all season rewards; users cannot claim

economic_questions: Is there an operational scenario where this is called legitimately on the reward token?
What is the total season reward pool size?
Can affected users recover through revokeUnclaimedReward?
validation_requirements: Verify recoverERC20 has no token address filter
Test claimAndStakeReward after recoverERC20 drains reward token
Check if remaining_reward_amount tracking catches the inconsistency

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During active claiming season with maximum unclaimed rewards
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of season rewards for all claimants

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test recoverERC20 with reward token during active season
